python.description: Python is the backbone of rapid prototyping. Dynamic typing makes it fast to write but watch your runtime errors â€” always type-hint your interfaces. I've debugged thousands of Python tracebacks. Read them bottom-up, check your imports, and never trust string concatenation in production.
python.relationship_to_npc: my primary language for scripting and automation

emotion_variants.friendly: Python? Love it. Great for getting things done fast. Let me know what you're building and I'll point you to the right libraries.
emotion_variants.annoyed: Python's fine. Just stop writing bare excepts and untyped dictionaries everywhere. Show me your traceback.

docker.description: Docker containers are how you ship reproducible environments. Multi-stage builds keep images lean. Alpine base images save space but watch out for missing glibc deps. Always pin your base image tags â€” never use :latest in production.
docker.relationship_to_npc: essential tooling I use for every deployment

emotion_variants.friendly: Docker's one of those tools that once you learn it, you can't imagine working without it. What's your setup look like?
emotion_variants.annoyed: Let me guess â€” you used :latest and now prod is broken. Pin your tags. Always.

git.description: Git is version control done right. Learn rebase, learn bisect, learn reflog â€” those three commands will save you more hours than any IDE plugin. Merge conflicts aren't scary if you understand the three-way merge. And for the love of clean history, squash your WIP commits before merging to main.
git.relationship_to_npc: the first tool I teach every junior developer

emotion_variants.friendly: Git's beautiful once it clicks. The mental model is just a directed acyclic graph of snapshots. What's tripping you up?
emotion_variants.annoyed: Force pushing to main? That's not a git problem, that's a process problem. Set up branch protection.

rest_api.description: REST is a set of constraints, not a protocol. Stateless, resource-oriented, proper HTTP verbs. GET is idempotent, POST is not. Use 201 for creation, 204 for no-content deletes, 422 for validation errors. Version your APIs from day one. HATEOAS is theoretically correct but practically overkill for most teams.
rest_api.relationship_to_npc: the interface pattern I've designed hundreds of times

emotion_variants.friendly: REST design is one of those things that seems simple until you have to handle pagination, filtering, and nested resources. Want me to walk through the patterns?
emotion_variants.annoyed: Putting verbs in your URLs defeats the entire purpose of REST. The HTTP method IS the verb.

kubernetes.description: Kubernetes is container orchestration at scale. Pods, services, deployments, ingress â€” learn those four resources and you cover 80% of use cases. Don't overthink it with custom operators until you've outgrown Helm charts. And always set resource limits â€” an unbounded pod will eat your entire node.
kubernetes.relationship_to_npc: the orchestration layer I deploy to in production

emotion_variants.friendly: K8s has a steep learning curve but once you get the abstractions, it's incredibly powerful. Start with a simple deployment and service, then layer on complexity.
emotion_variants.annoyed: Running Kubernetes for a single-container app is like hiring a symphony orchestra to play a ringtone. Use docker-compose.

ci_cd.description: Continuous integration catches bugs before they merge. Continuous deployment ships them automatically once they pass. Your pipeline should lint, test, build, and deploy â€” in that order. Keep build times under 10 minutes or developers will stop waiting for them. Cache your dependencies aggressively.
ci_cd.relationship_to_npc: the automation backbone I build for every team

emotion_variants.friendly: A good CI/CD pipeline is like having a quality gate that never sleeps. What's your current setup? GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI?
emotion_variants.annoyed: Deploying manually in 2026? Set up a pipeline. Even a basic one is better than 'it works on my machine.'

sql.description: SQL is the lingua franca of structured data. Master JOINs, understand query plans with EXPLAIN, index your WHERE clauses. N+1 queries are the number one performance killer in web apps. Use CTEs for readability, window functions for analytics. And normalize your schema â€” denormalize only when you have benchmarks proving you need to.
sql.relationship_to_npc: the query language I've optimized countless times

emotion_variants.friendly: SQL's been around since the 70s and it's still the best way to talk to structured data. What database are you running?
emotion_variants.annoyed: SELECT * in production? With no LIMIT? That's how you bring down a database.

authentication.description: Auth is the part of your system where mistakes cost the most. Use bcrypt or argon2 for password hashing â€” never MD5, never SHA1 alone. JWT for stateless auth but watch your expiration times. OAuth2 for third-party. CORS exists for a reason â€” understand it before you set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to *.
authentication.relationship_to_npc: the security layer I never let people shortcut

emotion_variants.friendly: Security's one of those things where doing it right from the start saves you from a disaster later. What's your auth flow look like?
emotion_variants.annoyed: Storing passwords in plaintext? I'm going to pretend I didn't hear that. Let's fix it now.

testing.description: Tests are documentation that runs. Unit tests verify logic, integration tests verify contracts, end-to-end tests verify workflows. Aim for 80% coverage but don't chase 100% â€” that last 20% is usually boilerplate getters. Mock external dependencies, never mock the thing you're testing. Parametrize your test cases.
testing.relationship_to_npc: the practice I insist on before any code merges

emotion_variants.friendly: Good tests give you confidence to refactor. What framework are you using? pytest, jest, go test?
emotion_variants.annoyed: 'It works on my machine' is not a test suite. Write the test. Run it in CI. Ship with confidence.

linux.description: Linux is the foundation. Everything in production runs on it. Learn the filesystem hierarchy, process management, systemd, networking basics â€” netstat, ss, iptables. Permissions matter: chmod 777 is never the answer. Use journalctl for logs, htop for processes, and strace when you're truly stuck.
linux.relationship_to_npc: the operating system I live in daily

emotion_variants.friendly: Linux is like a Swiss army knife â€” there's a tool for everything if you know where to look. What are you trying to do?
emotion_variants.annoyed: chmod 777? That's not fixing the problem, that's removing all security and hoping for the best.

microservices.description: Microservices solve organizational scaling, not technical scaling. Each service owns its data and its deployment. Communicate via async messaging where possible, sync REST only when you need immediate responses. If your team is under 20 people, a well-structured monolith will outperform microservices every time. Distributed systems are hard â€” don't add that complexity without a reason.
microservices.relationship_to_npc: an architecture pattern I recommend carefully and conditionally

emotion_variants.friendly: Microservices can be powerful when you have the team size and operational maturity to support them. Tell me about your team and I'll give you an honest take.
emotion_variants.annoyed: You have 3 developers and want 12 microservices? That's a distributed monolith with network latency. Start with a modular monolith.

debugging_methodology.description: Debugging is systematic elimination, not guessing. Reproduce the bug first â€” if you can't reproduce it, you can't fix it. Then isolate: binary search through your code path until you find the divergence between expected and actual. Read the error message. Read it again. 90% of the time the answer is in the stack trace. Use breakpoints, not print statements â€” unless you're debugging a race condition, then print is your friend.
debugging_methodology.relationship_to_npc: the skill I value above all others in engineering

emotion_variants.friendly: Debugging is detective work â€” and it's honestly the most satisfying part of engineering when you crack it. Walk me through what you're seeing.
emotion_variants.annoyed: You changed three things at once and now it's broken? Revert to the last working state and change one thing at a time.

networking.description: TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP â€” these are the primitives everything runs on. If your app can't connect, check DNS first, then firewall rules, then the service itself. Use dig for DNS, curl for HTTP, telnet or nc for raw TCP. Most 'the server is down' reports are actually DNS or routing issues. Learn to read packet captures with tcpdump when all else fails.
networking.relationship_to_npc: the layer I troubleshoot when everything else looks correct

emotion_variants.friendly: Networking issues are the most frustrating to debug because the failure is between two systems. Let's trace the path step by step.
emotion_variants.annoyed: It's always DNS. Check DNS first. Save yourself an hour.

performance.description: Profile before you optimize â€” gut feelings about performance are wrong more often than right. Measure latency at percentiles, not averages. p99 matters more than p50. Common wins: add an index, add a cache, reduce N+1 queries, batch your I/O. Premature optimization is the root of all evil, but known bottlenecks should be addressed immediately.
performance.relationship_to_npc: the discipline I apply only after measuring

emotion_variants.friendly: Performance work is satisfying â€” there's always a bottleneck to find and a measurable improvement to prove. What are you seeing that's slow?
emotion_variants.annoyed: You're optimizing a function that runs once at startup while your hot path does 10,000 unindexed queries? Priorities.
